---
title: "Quality Control"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
BiocManager::install("microbiomeDataSets")
library(microbiomeDataSets)


tse <- baboongut()
tse

library(mia)
# mia provides the summary() function for TreeSummarizedExperiment objects which returns the summary of counts for all samples and features including measures of central tendency.
summary(tse, assay.type = "counts")
#The returned tables show that samples exhibit lots of variance in library sizes. Moreover, we can observe that there are no singletons in the dataset

df <- summarizeDominance(tse, rank = "Genus")
df

#Another type of summary can be generated using the summarizeDominance() function. This function returns a table displaying both the absolute and relative abundance of each taxon — that is, how many times a taxon was detected in the dataset and the proportion of samples in which it was identified. Below, we create a summary table for genera.

uniq <- getUnique(tse, rank = "Phylum")
uniq #There are 13 phyla present in the dataset.


#Next, we might be interested in assessing which features are the most abundant. This can be done by utilizing the getTop() function.

top_features <- getTop(tse, method = "median", top = 10) #we pick the top 10 taxonomic features which is determined based on median abundance.
top_features #These ten features have the highest median abundance across all samples

prev <- getPrevalent(tse, rank = "Genus", prevalence = 0.2, detection = 0) #it retrieves which features exceeds the specified prevalence and detection thresholds.
prev

#getRare() function is counterpart of getPrevalent(). It returns those features that do not exceed the thresholds.
rare <- getRare(tse, rank = "Genus", prevalence = 0.2, detection = 0)

rare |> head()
```

```{r}
#Outliers, singletons and contaminants

library(scater)
tse <- addPerCellQC(tse)

library(miaViz)
library(patchwork)
p1 <- plotColData(tse, x = "sex", y = "total", colour_by = "age:")
p2 <- plotHistogram(tse, col.var = "total")
p1 +p2

# The distribution of library size is right-skewed. Most of the samples follow normal distribution while some of the samples deviates from this. This might be by technical variations or biological factors. 
# The most imortant check is to ensure tha the sampling depth is sufficient. In case of insufficient sampling depth, one might consider filtering the data based on library size (Section 9.4).
```

```{r}
#Contaminant sequences
library(knitr)

colData(tse) |>
    head() |>
    kable()

# Now we detect dontamiant sequences.

#tse <- addContaminantQC(tse, method = "frequency", concentration = "post_pcr_dna_ng")

# Keep only samples where concentration is not NA
tse_filtered <- tse[ , !is.na(colData(tse)$post_pcr_dna_ng)]

# Then run the QC
tse_filtered <- addContaminantQC(tse_filtered, concentration = "post_pcr_dna_ng")

rowData(tse) |> 
  head() |>
  kable()

tse_filtered<- tse_filtered[!rowData(tse_filtered)[["contaminant"]], ]
tse_filtered
```

```{r}
#Here is how we apply the prevalence based approach
control <- rep(FALSE, ncol(tse))
control[sample(seq_len(ncol(tse)),1)] <- TRUE
tse[["control"]] <- control

#Next, we perform the analysis. Note that we could have applied both frequency-based and prevalence-based methods simultaneously by specifying the method parameter in the previous step.

not_contaminant <- isNotContaminant(tse, control = "control", detailed = TRUE) #detailed = FALSE for results as a vector
not_contaminant |> head()
```

```{r}
plotHistogram(tse, assay.type = "counts")
#The microbiome data is typically zero-inflated, meaning that there are lots of zeroes. The same method can also be used to visualize continuous variables from colData. For categorical values, one can utilize plotBarplot().

p1 <- plotHistogram(tse, col.var = "age") +
    labs(x = "Age")
p2 <- plotBarplot(tse, col.var = "sex") +
    labs(x = "Sex")

p1 + p2
```
```{r}
# Abundance viz
#plotAbundanceDensity() function generates a plot to visualize the most abundant taxonomic features along with several options.
#Relative abundance = the proportion of a taxon in a sample, expressed as a percentage or fraction of the total counts.
tse <- transformAssay(tse, method = "relabundance")
plotAbundanceDensity(
  tse,
  layout = "jitter",
  assay.type = "relabundance",
  n = 40, point.size = 1, point.shape = 19,
  point.alpha = 0.1
) + 
      scale_x_log10(label = scales::percent)
# Each dot in a row represents the relative abundance of the the taxon in that specific sample

plotAbundanceDensity(
    tse,
    layout = "density",
    assay.type = "relabundance",
    n = 5, colour.by = "sex",
    point.alpha = 0.1
) +
    scale_x_log10() #Distribution of the relative abundance across all samples. Each panel represent one taxon.

# Select top features
top <- getTop(tse, top = 10L, method = "mean")

plotExpression(
    tse,
    features = top,
    x = "sex",
    assay.type = "relabundance",
    point_alpha = 0.01
) +
    scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
#Prevalence 
#quantifies the frequency of samples where certain microbes were detected (above a given detection threshold). Prevalence can be given as sample size (N) or percentage (unit interval).
#Investigating prevalence allows you either to focus on changes which pertain to the majority of the samples, or identify rare microbes, which may be conditionally abundant in a small number of samples.

#We can plot a histogram of the prevalence of features. This would tell us whether there are many features present in most of the samples or if there are mostly rare taxonomic features. The population prevalence (frequency) at a 0.01% relative abundance threshold (detection = 0.1/100 and as.relative = TRUE) can look like this.

# Add prevalence of features
tse <- addPrevalence(tse, detection = 0.1 / 100, as.relative = TRUE)

# Plot them with a histogram
plotHistogram(tse, row.var = "prevalence")


#vMost of the features are present only in a minority of the samples with a specified abundance threshold. Similar conclusion can be made with visualizations generated with the plotPrevalentAbundance() or plotRowPrevalence() functions.

p1 <- plotPrevalentAbundance(tse, as.relative = TRUE)

# Remove y axis text as there are so many features that one cannot read them
p2 <- plotRowPrevalence(tse, as.relative = TRUE) +
    theme(
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
    )

p1 + p2

#The plots above show that most of the taxonomic features are present with low abundance. However, there are a couple features that are both highly abundant and prevalent in the dataset.

#The figures also show the overall trend that most microbes are low in abundance and occur in a limited number of samples. This is a typical pattern in microbiome datasets, and can be further visualized as follows.

plotPrevalence(tse,as.relative = TRUE)

#The plot shows the relationship between microbial relative abundance and prevalence across samples. Features with higher prevalence (yellow) tend to have lower relative abundance, while less prevalent features (purple) can still reach higher abundances in some cases.

#To analyze how much the core taxonomic features are present in samples, we can calculate how large proportions the core features present in each sample. We can then visualize this distribution again with a histogram.

# Calculate the proportion of core taxa
tse <- addPrevalentAbundance(tse, prevalence = 50 / 100, detection = 0.1 / 100)

# Visualize
plotHistogram(tse, col.var = "prevalent_abundance")
```

```{r}
# Exercise 
library(mia)
library(miaViz)
library(scater)
library(patchwork)

# Step 1 - Load peerj13075 dataset
data("peerj13075", package = "mia")
tse <- peerj13075

# First look at the data
tse
colData(tse) |> head()
```

```{r}
#Summarise the counts with Histogram
plotHistogram(tse, assay.type = "counts") +
    labs(x = "Counts")
#Add prevalence to RowData
tse <- addPrevalence(tse, detection = 0.1/100, as.relative = TRUE)

# Verify it was added
rowData(tse) |> head()

#Visualize prevalence distribution
plotHistogram(tse, row.var = "prevalence")
#Interpretation: If the histogram spikes on the left, most taxa are rare (found in few samples). If it spikes on the right, most taxa are core/prevalent.

#Add library sizes to colData
tse <- addPerCellQC(tse)
colData(tse) |> head()

plotHistogram(tse, col.var = "total")
#Interpretation: A right-skewed histogram means some samples have much higher sequencing depth than others — normalization will be needed

#Bar plot of categorical colData values 
plotBarplot(tse, col.var = "Geographical_location")

#get the available taxonomy ranks
taxonomyRanks(tse)

#Summarize dominance of genera
dom <- summarizeDominance(tse, rank = "genus")
dom
## The top row shows the most dominant genus:
# - n = number of samples it dominates in
# - rel_freq = proportion of samples (multiply by 100 for %)

#Get the most orevalent features at Genus rank
# Using counts, prevalence threshold 20%, detection threshold 1
prev_features <- getPrevalent(
    tse,
    rank = "genus",
    prevalence = 0.2,      # 20%
    detection = 1,          # at least 1 count
    as.relative = FALSE     # use raw counts
)
prev_features

#Get most abundant features by median
top_features <- getTop(tse, method = "median", top = 10)
top_features 

#How does this differ from prevalent features?

#getPrevalent → finds taxa present in many samples (even at low abundance)
#getTop → finds taxa with the highest abundance (even if only in a few samples)
#A taxon can be highly abundant but rare (present in few samples), or very prevalent but at low abundance — they measure different things

#Visualize most prevalent abundance
tse <- transformAssay(tse, method = "relabundance")

# Jitter plot of top 20 prevalent features
plotAbundanceDensity(
    tse,
    layout = "jitter",
    assay.type = "relabundance",
    n = 20,
    point.size = 1,
    point.alpha = 0.1
) +
    scale_x_log10(label = scales::percent)

plotPrevalentAbundance(tse, as.relative = TRUE)
```
